{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01HhMH3wqfsKNFZfx2esS1zQ","type":"tool_result","content":"{\"content\":\"1: /*\\n2:  * Licensed to the Apache Software Foundation (ASF) under one or more\\n3:  * contributor license agreements. See the NOTICE file distributed with\\n4:  * this work for additional information regarding copyright ownership.\\n5:  * The ASF licenses this file to You under the Apache License, Version 2.0\\n6:  * (the \\\"License\\\"); you may not use this file except in compliance with\\n7:  * the License. You may obtain a copy of the License at\\n8:  *\\n9:  *    http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0\\n10:  *\\n11:  * Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software\\n12:  * distributed under the License is distributed on an \\\"AS IS\\\" BASIS,\\n13:  * WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.\\n14:  * See the License for the specific language governing permissions and\\n15:  * limitations under the License.\\n16:  */\\n17: package org.apache.kafka.clients.consumer;\\n18: \\n19: import org.apache.kafka.common.TopicPartition;\\n20: \\n21: import java.util.Collection;\\n22: \\n23: /**\\n24:  * A callback interface that the user can implement to trigger custom actions when the set of partitions assigned to the\\n25:  * consumer changes.\\n26:  * <p>\\n27:  * This is applicable when the consumer is having Kafka auto-manage group membership. If the consumer directly assigns partitions,\\n28:  * those partitions will never be reassigned and this callback is not applicable.\\n29:  * <p>\\n30:  * When Kafka is managing the group membership, a partition re-assignment will be triggered any time the members of the group change or the subscription\\n31:  * of the members changes. This can occur when processes die, new process instances are added or old instances come back to life after failure.\\n32:  * Partition re-assignments can also be triggered by changes affecting the subscribed topics (e.g. when the number of partitions is\\n33:  * administratively adjusted).\\n34:  * <p>\\n35:  * There are many uses for this functionality. One common use is saving offsets in a custom store. By saving offsets in\\n36:  * the {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} call we can ensure that any time partition assignment changes\\n37:  * the offset gets saved.\\n38:  * <p>\\n39:  * Another use is flushing out any kind of cache of intermediate results the consumer may be keeping. For example,\\n40:  * consider a case where the consumer is subscribed to a topic containing user page views, and the goal is to count the\\n41:  * number of page views per user for each five minute window. Let's say the topic is partitioned by the user id so that\\n42:  * all events for a particular user go to a single consumer instance. The consumer can keep in memory a running\\n43:  * tally of actions per user and only flush these out to a remote data store when its cache gets too big. However if a\\n44:  * partition is reassigned it may want to automatically trigger a flush of this cache, before the new owner takes over\\n45:  * consumption.\\n46:  * <p>\\n47:  * This callback will only execute in the user thread as part of the {@link Consumer#poll(java.time.Duration) poll(long)} call\\n48:  * whenever partition assignment changes.\\n49:  * <p>\\n50:  * Under normal conditions, if a partition is reassigned from one consumer to another, then the old consumer will\\n51:  * always invoke {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection) onPartitionsRevoked} for that partition prior to the new consumer\\n52:  * invoking {@link #onPartitionsAssigned(Collection) onPartitionsAssigned} for the same partition. So if offsets or other state is saved in the\\n53:  * {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection) onPartitionsRevoked} call by one consumer member, it will always be accessible by the time the\\n54:  * other consumer member taking over that partition and triggering its {@link #onPartitionsAssigned(Collection) onPartitionsAssigned} callback to load the state.\\n55:  * <p>\\n56:  * You can think of revocation as a graceful way to give up ownership of a partition. In some cases, the consumer may not have an opportunity to do so.\\n57:  * For example, if the session times out, then the partitions may be reassigned before we have a chance to revoke them gracefully.\\n58:  * For this case, we have a third callback {@link #onPartitionsLost(Collection)}. The difference between this function and\\n59:  * {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} is that upon invocation of {@link #onPartitionsLost(Collection)}, the partitions\\n60:  * may already be owned by some other members in the group and therefore users would not be able to commit its consumed offsets for example.\\n61:  * Users could implement these two functions differently (by default,\\n62:  * {@link #onPartitionsLost(Collection)} will be calling {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} directly); for example, in the\\n63:  * {@link #onPartitionsLost(Collection)} we should not need to store the offsets since we know these partitions are no longer owned by the consumer\\n64:  * at that time.\\n65:  * <p>\\n66:  * During a rebalance event, the {@link #onPartitionsAssigned(Collection) onPartitionsAssigned} function will always be triggered exactly once when\\n67:  * the rebalance completes. That is, even if there is no newly assigned partitions for a consumer member, its {@link #onPartitionsAssigned(Collection) onPartitionsAssigned}\\n68:  * will still be triggered with an empty collection of partitions. As a result this function can be used also to notify when a rebalance event has happened.\\n69:  * With eager rebalancing, {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} will always be called at the start of a rebalance. On the other hand, {@link #onPartitionsLost(Collection)}\\n70:  * will only be called when there were non-empty partitions that were lost.\\n71:  * With cooperative rebalancing, {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} and {@link #onPartitionsLost(Collection)}\\n72:  * will only be triggered when there are non-empty partitions revoked or lost from this consumer member during a rebalance event.\\n73:  * <p>\\n74:  * It is possible\\n75:  * for a {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException} or {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException}\\n76:  * to be raised from one of these nested invocations. In this case, the exception will be propagated to the current\\n77:  * invocation of {@link KafkaConsumer#poll(java.time.Duration)} in which this callback is being executed. This means it is not\\n78:  * necessary to catch these exceptions and re-attempt to wakeup or interrupt the consumer thread.\\n79:  * Also if the callback function implementation itself throws an exception, this exception will be propagated to the current\\n80:  * invocation of {@link KafkaConsumer#poll(java.time.Duration)} as well.\\n81:  * <p>\\n82:  * Note that callbacks only serve as notification of an assignment change.\\n83:  * They cannot be used to express acceptance of the change.\\n84:  * Hence throwing an exception from a callback does not affect the assignment in any way,\\n85:  * as it will be propagated all the way up to the {@link KafkaConsumer#poll(java.time.Duration)} call.\\n86:  * If user captures the exception in the caller, the callback is still assumed successful and no further retries will be attempted.\\n87:  * <p>\\n88:  *\\n89:  * Here is pseudo-code for a callback implementation for saving offsets:\\n90:  * <pre>\\n91:  * {@code\\n92:  *   public class SaveOffsetsOnRebalance implements ConsumerRebalanceListener {\\n93:  *       private Consumer<?,?> consumer;\\n94:  *\\n95:  *       public SaveOffsetsOnRebalance(Consumer<?,?> consumer) {\\n96:  *           this.consumer = consumer;\\n97:  *       }\\n98:  *\\n99:  *       public void onPartitionsRevoked(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions) {\\n100:  *           // save the offsets in an external store using some custom code not described here\\n101:  *           for(TopicPartition partition: partitions)\\n102:  *              saveOffsetInExternalStore(consumer.position(partition));\\n103:  *       }\\n104:  *\\n105:  *       public void onPartitionsLost(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions) {\\n106:  *           // do not need to save the offsets since these partitions are probably owned by other consumers already\\n107:  *       }\\n108:  *\\n109:  *       public void onPartitionsAssigned(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions) {\\n110:  *           // read the offsets from an external store using some custom code not described here\\n111:  *           for(TopicPartition partition: partitions)\\n112:  *              consumer.seek(partition, readOffsetFromExternalStore(partition));\\n113:  *       }\\n114:  *   }\\n115:  * }\\n116:  * </pre>\\n117:  */\\n118: public interface ConsumerRebalanceListener {\\n119: \\n120:     /**\\n121:      * A callback method the user can implement to provide handling of offset commits to a customized store.\\n122:      * This method will be called during a rebalance operation when the consumer has to give up some partitions.\\n123:      * The consumer may need to give up some partitions (thus this callback executed) under the following scenarios:\\n124:      * <ul>\\n125:      *     <li>If the consumer assignment changes</li>\\n126:      *     <li>If the consumer is being closed ({@link KafkaConsumer#close(CloseOptions option)})</li>\\n127:      *     <li>If the consumer is unsubscribing ({@link KafkaConsumer#unsubscribe()})</li>\\n128:      * </ul>\\n129:      * It is recommended that offsets should be committed in this callback to either Kafka or a\\n130:      * custom offset store to prevent duplicate data.\\n131:      * <p>\\n132:      * This callback is always called before re-assigning the partitions.\\n133:      * If the consumer is using the {@link GroupProtocol#CLASSIC} rebalance protocol:\\n134:      * <ul>\\n135:      *     <li>\\n136:      *         In eager rebalancing, onPartitionsRevoked will be called with the full set of assigned partitions as a parameter (all partitions are revoked).\\n137:      *         It will be called even if there are no partitions to revoke.\\n138:      *     </li>\\n139:      *     <li>\\n140:      *         In cooperative rebalancing, onPartitionsRevoked will be called with the set of partitions to revoke,\\n141:      *         iff the set is non-empty.\\n142:      *     </li>\\n143:      * </ul>\\n144:      * If the consumer is using the {@link GroupProtocol#CONSUMER} rebalance protocol, this callback will be called\\n145:      * with the set of partitions to revoke iff the set is non-empty\\n146:      * (same behavior as the {@link GroupProtocol#CLASSIC} rebalance protocol with Cooperative mode).\\n147:      * <p>\\n148:      * For examples on usage of this API, see Usage Examples section of {@link KafkaConsumer KafkaConsumer}.\\n149:      * <p>\\n150:      * It is common for the revocation callback to use the consumer instance in order to commit offsets. It is possible\\n151:      * for a {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException} or {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException}\\n152:      * to be raised from one of these nested invocations. In this case, the exception will be propagated to the current\\n153:      * invocation of {@link KafkaConsumer#poll(java.time.Duration)} in which this callback is being executed. This means it is not\\n154:      * necessary to catch these exceptions and re-attempt to wakeup or interrupt the consumer thread.\\n155:      *\\n156:      * @param partitions The list of partitions that were assigned to the consumer and now need to be revoked. This will\\n157:      *                  include the full assignment under the Classic/Eager protocol, given that it revokes all partitions.\\n158:      *                   It will only include the subset to revoke under the Classic/Cooperative and Consumer protocols.\\n159:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException If raised from a nested call to {@link KafkaConsumer}\\n160:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException If raised from a nested call to {@link KafkaConsumer}\\n161:      */\\n162:     void onPartitionsRevoked(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions);\\n163: \\n164:     /**\\n165:      * A callback method the user can implement to provide handling of customized offsets on completion of a successful\\n166:      * partition re-assignment. This method will be called after the partition re-assignment completes (even if no new\\n167:      * partitions were assigned to the consumer), and before the consumer starts fetching data,\\n168:      * and only as the result of a {@link Consumer#poll(java.time.Duration) poll(long)} call.\\n169:      * <p>\\n170:      * It is guaranteed that under normal conditions all the processes in a consumer group will execute their\\n171:      * {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} callback before any instance executes this onPartitionsAssigned callback.\\n172:      * During exceptional scenarios, partitions may be migrated\\n173:      * without the old owner being notified (i.e. their {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} callback not triggered),\\n174:      * and later when the old owner consumer realized this event, the {@link #onPartitionsLost(Collection)} callback\\n175:      * will be triggered by the consumer then.\\n176:      * <p>\\n177:      * It is common for the assignment callback to use the consumer instance in order to query offsets. It is possible\\n178:      * for a {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException} or {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException}\\n179:      * to be raised from one of these nested invocations. In this case, the exception will be propagated to the current\\n180:      * invocation of {@link KafkaConsumer#poll(java.time.Duration)} in which this callback is being executed. This means it is not\\n181:      * necessary to catch these exceptions and re-attempt to wakeup or interrupt the consumer thread.\\n182:      *\\n183:      * @param partitions Partitions that have been added to the assignment as a result of the rebalance.\\n184:      *                   Note that partitions that were already owned by this consumer and remain assigned are not\\n185:      *                   included in this list under the Classic/Cooperative or Consumer protocols. THe full assignment\\n186:      *                   will be received under the Classic/Eager protocol.\\n187:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException    If raised from a nested call to {@link KafkaConsumer}\\n188:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException If raised from a nested call to {@link KafkaConsumer}\\n189:      */\\n190:     void onPartitionsAssigned(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions);\\n191: \\n192:     /**\\n193:      * A callback method you can implement to provide handling of cleaning up resources for partitions that have already\\n194:      * been reassigned to other consumers. This method will not be called during normal execution as the owned partitions would\\n195:      * first be revoked by calling the {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener#onPartitionsRevoked}, before being reassigned\\n196:      * to other consumers during a rebalance event. However, during exceptional scenarios when the consumer realized that it\\n197:      * does not own this partition any longer, i.e. not revoked via a normal rebalance event, then this method would be invoked.\\n198:      * <p>\\n199:      * For example, this function is called if a consumer's session timeout has expired, or if a fatal error has been\\n200:      * received indicating the consumer is no longer part of the group.\\n201:      * <p>\\n202:      * By default it will just trigger {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener#onPartitionsRevoked}; for users who want to distinguish\\n203:      * the handling logic of revoked partitions v.s. lost partitions, they can override the default implementation.\\n204:      * <p>\\n205:      * It is possible\\n206:      * for a {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException} or {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException}\\n207:      * to be raised from one of these nested invocations. In this case, the exception will be propagated to the current\\n208:      * invocation of {@link KafkaConsumer#poll(java.time.Duration)} in which this callback is being executed. This means it is not\\n209:      * necessary to catch these exceptions and re-attempt to wakeup or interrupt the consumer thread.\\n210:      *\\n211:      * @param partitions The list of partitions that were assigned to the consumer and now have been reassigned\\n212:      *                   to other consumers. With both, the Classic and Consumer protocols, this will always include\\n213:      *                   all partitions that were previously assigned to the consumer.\\n214:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException    If raised from a nested call to {@link KafkaConsumer}\\n215:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException If raised from a nested call to {@link KafkaConsumer}\\n216:      */\\n217:     default void onPartitionsLost(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions) {\\n218:         onPartitionsRevoked(partitions);\\n219:     }\\n220: }\\n221: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-benchmarks/kafka--e678b4b@47ac12bd6042edf23f1dbca3d789db945d62802d/-/blob/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/consumer/ConsumerRebalanceListener.java\"}"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"62ce8e1e-8771-415c-ac7e-3a2a25914119","uuid":"3f2f5c5a-2a8f-4fee-a50f-966d1c408d03","tool_use_result":{"content":"{\"content\":\"1: /*\\n2:  * Licensed to the Apache Software Foundation (ASF) under one or more\\n3:  * contributor license agreements. See the NOTICE file distributed with\\n4:  * this work for additional information regarding copyright ownership.\\n5:  * The ASF licenses this file to You under the Apache License, Version 2.0\\n6:  * (the \\\"License\\\"); you may not use this file except in compliance with\\n7:  * the License. You may obtain a copy of the License at\\n8:  *\\n9:  *    http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0\\n10:  *\\n11:  * Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software\\n12:  * distributed under the License is distributed on an \\\"AS IS\\\" BASIS,\\n13:  * WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.\\n14:  * See the License for the specific language governing permissions and\\n15:  * limitations under the License.\\n16:  */\\n17: package org.apache.kafka.clients.consumer;\\n18: \\n19: import org.apache.kafka.common.TopicPartition;\\n20: \\n21: import java.util.Collection;\\n22: \\n23: /**\\n24:  * A callback interface that the user can implement to trigger custom actions when the set of partitions assigned to the\\n25:  * consumer changes.\\n26:  * <p>\\n27:  * This is applicable when the consumer is having Kafka auto-manage group membership. If the consumer directly assigns partitions,\\n28:  * those partitions will never be reassigned and this callback is not applicable.\\n29:  * <p>\\n30:  * When Kafka is managing the group membership, a partition re-assignment will be triggered any time the members of the group change or the subscription\\n31:  * of the members changes. This can occur when processes die, new process instances are added or old instances come back to life after failure.\\n32:  * Partition re-assignments can also be triggered by changes affecting the subscribed topics (e.g. when the number of partitions is\\n33:  * administratively adjusted).\\n34:  * <p>\\n35:  * There are many uses for this functionality. One common use is saving offsets in a custom store. By saving offsets in\\n36:  * the {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} call we can ensure that any time partition assignment changes\\n37:  * the offset gets saved.\\n38:  * <p>\\n39:  * Another use is flushing out any kind of cache of intermediate results the consumer may be keeping. For example,\\n40:  * consider a case where the consumer is subscribed to a topic containing user page views, and the goal is to count the\\n41:  * number of page views per user for each five minute window. Let's say the topic is partitioned by the user id so that\\n42:  * all events for a particular user go to a single consumer instance. The consumer can keep in memory a running\\n43:  * tally of actions per user and only flush these out to a remote data store when its cache gets too big. However if a\\n44:  * partition is reassigned it may want to automatically trigger a flush of this cache, before the new owner takes over\\n45:  * consumption.\\n46:  * <p>\\n47:  * This callback will only execute in the user thread as part of the {@link Consumer#poll(java.time.Duration) poll(long)} call\\n48:  * whenever partition assignment changes.\\n49:  * <p>\\n50:  * Under normal conditions, if a partition is reassigned from one consumer to another, then the old consumer will\\n51:  * always invoke {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection) onPartitionsRevoked} for that partition prior to the new consumer\\n52:  * invoking {@link #onPartitionsAssigned(Collection) onPartitionsAssigned} for the same partition. So if offsets or other state is saved in the\\n53:  * {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection) onPartitionsRevoked} call by one consumer member, it will always be accessible by the time the\\n54:  * other consumer member taking over that partition and triggering its {@link #onPartitionsAssigned(Collection) onPartitionsAssigned} callback to load the state.\\n55:  * <p>\\n56:  * You can think of revocation as a graceful way to give up ownership of a partition. In some cases, the consumer may not have an opportunity to do so.\\n57:  * For example, if the session times out, then the partitions may be reassigned before we have a chance to revoke them gracefully.\\n58:  * For this case, we have a third callback {@link #onPartitionsLost(Collection)}. The difference between this function and\\n59:  * {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} is that upon invocation of {@link #onPartitionsLost(Collection)}, the partitions\\n60:  * may already be owned by some other members in the group and therefore users would not be able to commit its consumed offsets for example.\\n61:  * Users could implement these two functions differently (by default,\\n62:  * {@link #onPartitionsLost(Collection)} will be calling {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} directly); for example, in the\\n63:  * {@link #onPartitionsLost(Collection)} we should not need to store the offsets since we know these partitions are no longer owned by the consumer\\n64:  * at that time.\\n65:  * <p>\\n66:  * During a rebalance event, the {@link #onPartitionsAssigned(Collection) onPartitionsAssigned} function will always be triggered exactly once when\\n67:  * the rebalance completes. That is, even if there is no newly assigned partitions for a consumer member, its {@link #onPartitionsAssigned(Collection) onPartitionsAssigned}\\n68:  * will still be triggered with an empty collection of partitions. As a result this function can be used also to notify when a rebalance event has happened.\\n69:  * With eager rebalancing, {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} will always be called at the start of a rebalance. On the other hand, {@link #onPartitionsLost(Collection)}\\n70:  * will only be called when there were non-empty partitions that were lost.\\n71:  * With cooperative rebalancing, {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} and {@link #onPartitionsLost(Collection)}\\n72:  * will only be triggered when there are non-empty partitions revoked or lost from this consumer member during a rebalance event.\\n73:  * <p>\\n74:  * It is possible\\n75:  * for a {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException} or {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException}\\n76:  * to be raised from one of these nested invocations. In this case, the exception will be propagated to the current\\n77:  * invocation of {@link KafkaConsumer#poll(java.time.Duration)} in which this callback is being executed. This means it is not\\n78:  * necessary to catch these exceptions and re-attempt to wakeup or interrupt the consumer thread.\\n79:  * Also if the callback function implementation itself throws an exception, this exception will be propagated to the current\\n80:  * invocation of {@link KafkaConsumer#poll(java.time.Duration)} as well.\\n81:  * <p>\\n82:  * Note that callbacks only serve as notification of an assignment change.\\n83:  * They cannot be used to express acceptance of the change.\\n84:  * Hence throwing an exception from a callback does not affect the assignment in any way,\\n85:  * as it will be propagated all the way up to the {@link KafkaConsumer#poll(java.time.Duration)} call.\\n86:  * If user captures the exception in the caller, the callback is still assumed successful and no further retries will be attempted.\\n87:  * <p>\\n88:  *\\n89:  * Here is pseudo-code for a callback implementation for saving offsets:\\n90:  * <pre>\\n91:  * {@code\\n92:  *   public class SaveOffsetsOnRebalance implements ConsumerRebalanceListener {\\n93:  *       private Consumer<?,?> consumer;\\n94:  *\\n95:  *       public SaveOffsetsOnRebalance(Consumer<?,?> consumer) {\\n96:  *           this.consumer = consumer;\\n97:  *       }\\n98:  *\\n99:  *       public void onPartitionsRevoked(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions) {\\n100:  *           // save the offsets in an external store using some custom code not described here\\n101:  *           for(TopicPartition partition: partitions)\\n102:  *              saveOffsetInExternalStore(consumer.position(partition));\\n103:  *       }\\n104:  *\\n105:  *       public void onPartitionsLost(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions) {\\n106:  *           // do not need to save the offsets since these partitions are probably owned by other consumers already\\n107:  *       }\\n108:  *\\n109:  *       public void onPartitionsAssigned(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions) {\\n110:  *           // read the offsets from an external store using some custom code not described here\\n111:  *           for(TopicPartition partition: partitions)\\n112:  *              consumer.seek(partition, readOffsetFromExternalStore(partition));\\n113:  *       }\\n114:  *   }\\n115:  * }\\n116:  * </pre>\\n117:  */\\n118: public interface ConsumerRebalanceListener {\\n119: \\n120:     /**\\n121:      * A callback method the user can implement to provide handling of offset commits to a customized store.\\n122:      * This method will be called during a rebalance operation when the consumer has to give up some partitions.\\n123:      * The consumer may need to give up some partitions (thus this callback executed) under the following scenarios:\\n124:      * <ul>\\n125:      *     <li>If the consumer assignment changes</li>\\n126:      *     <li>If the consumer is being closed ({@link KafkaConsumer#close(CloseOptions option)})</li>\\n127:      *     <li>If the consumer is unsubscribing ({@link KafkaConsumer#unsubscribe()})</li>\\n128:      * </ul>\\n129:      * It is recommended that offsets should be committed in this callback to either Kafka or a\\n130:      * custom offset store to prevent duplicate data.\\n131:      * <p>\\n132:      * This callback is always called before re-assigning the partitions.\\n133:      * If the consumer is using the {@link GroupProtocol#CLASSIC} rebalance protocol:\\n134:      * <ul>\\n135:      *     <li>\\n136:      *         In eager rebalancing, onPartitionsRevoked will be called with the full set of assigned partitions as a parameter (all partitions are revoked).\\n137:      *         It will be called even if there are no partitions to revoke.\\n138:      *     </li>\\n139:      *     <li>\\n140:      *         In cooperative rebalancing, onPartitionsRevoked will be called with the set of partitions to revoke,\\n141:      *         iff the set is non-empty.\\n142:      *     </li>\\n143:      * </ul>\\n144:      * If the consumer is using the {@link GroupProtocol#CONSUMER} rebalance protocol, this callback will be called\\n145:      * with the set of partitions to revoke iff the set is non-empty\\n146:      * (same behavior as the {@link GroupProtocol#CLASSIC} rebalance protocol with Cooperative mode).\\n147:      * <p>\\n148:      * For examples on usage of this API, see Usage Examples section of {@link KafkaConsumer KafkaConsumer}.\\n149:      * <p>\\n150:      * It is common for the revocation callback to use the consumer instance in order to commit offsets. It is possible\\n151:      * for a {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException} or {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException}\\n152:      * to be raised from one of these nested invocations. In this case, the exception will be propagated to the current\\n153:      * invocation of {@link KafkaConsumer#poll(java.time.Duration)} in which this callback is being executed. This means it is not\\n154:      * necessary to catch these exceptions and re-attempt to wakeup or interrupt the consumer thread.\\n155:      *\\n156:      * @param partitions The list of partitions that were assigned to the consumer and now need to be revoked. This will\\n157:      *                  include the full assignment under the Classic/Eager protocol, given that it revokes all partitions.\\n158:      *                   It will only include the subset to revoke under the Classic/Cooperative and Consumer protocols.\\n159:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException If raised from a nested call to {@link KafkaConsumer}\\n160:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException If raised from a nested call to {@link KafkaConsumer}\\n161:      */\\n162:     void onPartitionsRevoked(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions);\\n163: \\n164:     /**\\n165:      * A callback method the user can implement to provide handling of customized offsets on completion of a successful\\n166:      * partition re-assignment. This method will be called after the partition re-assignment completes (even if no new\\n167:      * partitions were assigned to the consumer), and before the consumer starts fetching data,\\n168:      * and only as the result of a {@link Consumer#poll(java.time.Duration) poll(long)} call.\\n169:      * <p>\\n170:      * It is guaranteed that under normal conditions all the processes in a consumer group will execute their\\n171:      * {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} callback before any instance executes this onPartitionsAssigned callback.\\n172:      * During exceptional scenarios, partitions may be migrated\\n173:      * without the old owner being notified (i.e. their {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} callback not triggered),\\n174:      * and later when the old owner consumer realized this event, the {@link #onPartitionsLost(Collection)} callback\\n175:      * will be triggered by the consumer then.\\n176:      * <p>\\n177:      * It is common for the assignment callback to use the consumer instance in order to query offsets. It is possible\\n178:      * for a {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException} or {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException}\\n179:      * to be raised from one of these nested invocations. In this case, the exception will be propagated to the current\\n180:      * invocation of {@link KafkaConsumer#poll(java.time.Duration)} in which this callback is being executed. This means it is not\\n181:      * necessary to catch these exceptions and re-attempt to wakeup or interrupt the consumer thread.\\n182:      *\\n183:      * @param partitions Partitions that have been added to the assignment as a result of the rebalance.\\n184:      *                   Note that partitions that were already owned by this consumer and remain assigned are not\\n185:      *                   included in this list under the Classic/Cooperative or Consumer protocols. THe full assignment\\n186:      *                   will be received under the Classic/Eager protocol.\\n187:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException    If raised from a nested call to {@link KafkaConsumer}\\n188:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException If raised from a nested call to {@link KafkaConsumer}\\n189:      */\\n190:     void onPartitionsAssigned(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions);\\n191: \\n192:     /**\\n193:      * A callback method you can implement to provide handling of cleaning up resources for partitions that have already\\n194:      * been reassigned to other consumers. This method will not be called during normal execution as the owned partitions would\\n195:      * first be revoked by calling the {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener#onPartitionsRevoked}, before being reassigned\\n196:      * to other consumers during a rebalance event. However, during exceptional scenarios when the consumer realized that it\\n197:      * does not own this partition any longer, i.e. not revoked via a normal rebalance event, then this method would be invoked.\\n198:      * <p>\\n199:      * For example, this function is called if a consumer's session timeout has expired, or if a fatal error has been\\n200:      * received indicating the consumer is no longer part of the group.\\n201:      * <p>\\n202:      * By default it will just trigger {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener#onPartitionsRevoked}; for users who want to distinguish\\n203:      * the handling logic of revoked partitions v.s. lost partitions, they can override the default implementation.\\n204:      * <p>\\n205:      * It is possible\\n206:      * for a {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException} or {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException}\\n207:      * to be raised from one of these nested invocations. In this case, the exception will be propagated to the current\\n208:      * invocation of {@link KafkaConsumer#poll(java.time.Duration)} in which this callback is being executed. This means it is not\\n209:      * necessary to catch these exceptions and re-attempt to wakeup or interrupt the consumer thread.\\n210:      *\\n211:      * @param partitions The list of partitions that were assigned to the consumer and now have been reassigned\\n212:      *                   to other consumers. With both, the Classic and Consumer protocols, this will always include\\n213:      *                   all partitions that were previously assigned to the consumer.\\n214:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException    If raised from a nested call to {@link KafkaConsumer}\\n215:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException If raised from a nested call to {@link KafkaConsumer}\\n216:      */\\n217:     default void onPartitionsLost(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions) {\\n218:         onPartitionsRevoked(partitions);\\n219:     }\\n220: }\\n221: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-benchmarks/kafka--e678b4b@47ac12bd6042edf23f1dbca3d789db945d62802d/-/blob/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/consumer/ConsumerRebalanceListener.java\"}","structuredContent":{"content":"1: /*\n2:  * Licensed to the Apache Software Foundation (ASF) under one or more\n3:  * contributor license agreements. See the NOTICE file distributed with\n4:  * this work for additional information regarding copyright ownership.\n5:  * The ASF licenses this file to You under the Apache License, Version 2.0\n6:  * (the \"License\"); you may not use this file except in compliance with\n7:  * the License. You may obtain a copy of the License at\n8:  *\n9:  *    http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0\n10:  *\n11:  * Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software\n12:  * distributed under the License is distributed on an \"AS IS\" BASIS,\n13:  * WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.\n14:  * See the License for the specific language governing permissions and\n15:  * limitations under the License.\n16:  */\n17: package org.apache.kafka.clients.consumer;\n18: \n19: import org.apache.kafka.common.TopicPartition;\n20: \n21: import java.util.Collection;\n22: \n23: /**\n24:  * A callback interface that the user can implement to trigger custom actions when the set of partitions assigned to the\n25:  * consumer changes.\n26:  * <p>\n27:  * This is applicable when the consumer is having Kafka auto-manage group membership. If the consumer directly assigns partitions,\n28:  * those partitions will never be reassigned and this callback is not applicable.\n29:  * <p>\n30:  * When Kafka is managing the group membership, a partition re-assignment will be triggered any time the members of the group change or the subscription\n31:  * of the members changes. This can occur when processes die, new process instances are added or old instances come back to life after failure.\n32:  * Partition re-assignments can also be triggered by changes affecting the subscribed topics (e.g. when the number of partitions is\n33:  * administratively adjusted).\n34:  * <p>\n35:  * There are many uses for this functionality. One common use is saving offsets in a custom store. By saving offsets in\n36:  * the {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} call we can ensure that any time partition assignment changes\n37:  * the offset gets saved.\n38:  * <p>\n39:  * Another use is flushing out any kind of cache of intermediate results the consumer may be keeping. For example,\n40:  * consider a case where the consumer is subscribed to a topic containing user page views, and the goal is to count the\n41:  * number of page views per user for each five minute window. Let's say the topic is partitioned by the user id so that\n42:  * all events for a particular user go to a single consumer instance. The consumer can keep in memory a running\n43:  * tally of actions per user and only flush these out to a remote data store when its cache gets too big. However if a\n44:  * partition is reassigned it may want to automatically trigger a flush of this cache, before the new owner takes over\n45:  * consumption.\n46:  * <p>\n47:  * This callback will only execute in the user thread as part of the {@link Consumer#poll(java.time.Duration) poll(long)} call\n48:  * whenever partition assignment changes.\n49:  * <p>\n50:  * Under normal conditions, if a partition is reassigned from one consumer to another, then the old consumer will\n51:  * always invoke {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection) onPartitionsRevoked} for that partition prior to the new consumer\n52:  * invoking {@link #onPartitionsAssigned(Collection) onPartitionsAssigned} for the same partition. So if offsets or other state is saved in the\n53:  * {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection) onPartitionsRevoked} call by one consumer member, it will always be accessible by the time the\n54:  * other consumer member taking over that partition and triggering its {@link #onPartitionsAssigned(Collection) onPartitionsAssigned} callback to load the state.\n55:  * <p>\n56:  * You can think of revocation as a graceful way to give up ownership of a partition. In some cases, the consumer may not have an opportunity to do so.\n57:  * For example, if the session times out, then the partitions may be reassigned before we have a chance to revoke them gracefully.\n58:  * For this case, we have a third callback {@link #onPartitionsLost(Collection)}. The difference between this function and\n59:  * {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} is that upon invocation of {@link #onPartitionsLost(Collection)}, the partitions\n60:  * may already be owned by some other members in the group and therefore users would not be able to commit its consumed offsets for example.\n61:  * Users could implement these two functions differently (by default,\n62:  * {@link #onPartitionsLost(Collection)} will be calling {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} directly); for example, in the\n63:  * {@link #onPartitionsLost(Collection)} we should not need to store the offsets since we know these partitions are no longer owned by the consumer\n64:  * at that time.\n65:  * <p>\n66:  * During a rebalance event, the {@link #onPartitionsAssigned(Collection) onPartitionsAssigned} function will always be triggered exactly once when\n67:  * the rebalance completes. That is, even if there is no newly assigned partitions for a consumer member, its {@link #onPartitionsAssigned(Collection) onPartitionsAssigned}\n68:  * will still be triggered with an empty collection of partitions. As a result this function can be used also to notify when a rebalance event has happened.\n69:  * With eager rebalancing, {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} will always be called at the start of a rebalance. On the other hand, {@link #onPartitionsLost(Collection)}\n70:  * will only be called when there were non-empty partitions that were lost.\n71:  * With cooperative rebalancing, {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} and {@link #onPartitionsLost(Collection)}\n72:  * will only be triggered when there are non-empty partitions revoked or lost from this consumer member during a rebalance event.\n73:  * <p>\n74:  * It is possible\n75:  * for a {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException} or {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException}\n76:  * to be raised from one of these nested invocations. In this case, the exception will be propagated to the current\n77:  * invocation of {@link KafkaConsumer#poll(java.time.Duration)} in which this callback is being executed. This means it is not\n78:  * necessary to catch these exceptions and re-attempt to wakeup or interrupt the consumer thread.\n79:  * Also if the callback function implementation itself throws an exception, this exception will be propagated to the current\n80:  * invocation of {@link KafkaConsumer#poll(java.time.Duration)} as well.\n81:  * <p>\n82:  * Note that callbacks only serve as notification of an assignment change.\n83:  * They cannot be used to express acceptance of the change.\n84:  * Hence throwing an exception from a callback does not affect the assignment in any way,\n85:  * as it will be propagated all the way up to the {@link KafkaConsumer#poll(java.time.Duration)} call.\n86:  * If user captures the exception in the caller, the callback is still assumed successful and no further retries will be attempted.\n87:  * <p>\n88:  *\n89:  * Here is pseudo-code for a callback implementation for saving offsets:\n90:  * <pre>\n91:  * {@code\n92:  *   public class SaveOffsetsOnRebalance implements ConsumerRebalanceListener {\n93:  *       private Consumer<?,?> consumer;\n94:  *\n95:  *       public SaveOffsetsOnRebalance(Consumer<?,?> consumer) {\n96:  *           this.consumer = consumer;\n97:  *       }\n98:  *\n99:  *       public void onPartitionsRevoked(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions) {\n100:  *           // save the offsets in an external store using some custom code not described here\n101:  *           for(TopicPartition partition: partitions)\n102:  *              saveOffsetInExternalStore(consumer.position(partition));\n103:  * other consumers already\n107:  *       }\n108:  *\n109:  *       public void onPartitionsAssigned(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions) {\n110:  *           // read the offsets from an external store using some custom code not described here\n111:  *           for(TopicPartition partition: partitions)\n112:  *              consumer.seek(partition, readOffsetFromExternalStore(partition));\n113:  *       }\n114:  *   }\n115:  * }\n116:  * </pre>\n117:  */\n118: public interface ConsumerRebalanceListener {\n119: \n120:     /**\n121:      * A callback method the user can implement to provide handling of offset commits to a customized store.\n122:      * This method will be called during a rebalance operation when the consumer has to give up some partitions.\n123:      * The consumer may need to give up some partitions (thus this callback executed) under the following scenarios:\n124:      * <ul>\n125:      *     <li>If the consumer assignment changes</li>\n126:      *     <li>If the consumer is being closed ({@link KafkaConsumer#close(CloseOptions option)})</li>\n127:      *     <li>If the consumer is unsubscribing ({@link KafkaConsumer#unsubscribe()})</li>\n128:      * </ul>\n129:      * It is recommended that offsets should be committed in this callback to either Kafka or a\n130:      * custom offset store to prevent duplicate data.\n131:      * <p>\n132:      * This callback is always called before re-assigning the partitions.\n133:      * If the consumer is using the {@link GroupProtocol#CLASSIC} rebalance protocol:\n134:      * <ul>\n135:      *     <li>\n136:      *         In eager rebalancing, onPartitionsRevoked will be called with the full set of assigned partitions as a parameter (all partitions are revoked).\n137:      *         It will be called even if there are no partitions to revoke.\n138:      *     </li>\n139:      *     <li>\n140:      *         In cooperative rebalancing, onPartitionsRevoked will be called with the set of partitions to revoke,\n141:      *         iff the set is non-empty.\n142:      *     </li>\n143:      * </ul>\n144:      * If the consumer is using the {@link GroupProtocol#CONSUMER} rebalance protocol, this callback will be called\n145:      * with the set of partitions to revoke iff the set is non-empty\n146:      * (same behavior as the {@link GroupProtocol#CLASSIC} rebalance protocol with Cooperative mode).\n147:      * <p>\n148:      * For examples on usage of this API, see Usage Examples section of {@link KafkaConsumer KafkaConsumer}.\n149:      * <p>\n150:      * It is common for the revocation callback to use the consumer instance in order to commit offsets. It is possible\n151:      * for a {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException} or {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException}\n152:      * to be raised from one of these nested invocations. In this case, the exception will be propagated to the current\n153:      * invocation of {@link KafkaConsumer#poll(java.time.Duration)} in which this callback is being executed. This means it is not\n154:      * necessary to catch these exceptions and re-attempt to wakeup or interrupt the consumer thread.\n155:      *\n156:      * @param partitions The list of partitions that were assigned to the consumer and now need to be revoked. This method will be called after the partition re-assignment completes (even if no new\n167:      * partitions were assigned to the consumer), and before the consumer starts fetching data,\n168:      * and only as the result of a {@link Consumer#poll(java.time.Duration) poll(long)} call.\n169:      * <p>\n170:      * It is guaranteed that under normal conditions all the processes in a consumer group will execute their\n171:      * {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} callback before any instance executes this onPartitionsAssigned callback.\n172:      * During exceptional scenarios, partitions may be migrated\n173:      * without the old owner being notified (i.e. their {@link #onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} callback not triggered),\n174:      * and later when the old owner consumer realized this event, the {@link #onPartitionsLost(Collection)} callback\n175:      * will be triggered by the consumer then.\n176:      * <p>\n177:      * It is common for the assignment callback to use the consumer instance in order to query offsets. It is possible\n178:      * for a {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException} or {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException}\n179:      * to be raised from one of these nested invocations. In this case, the exception will be propagated to the current\n180:      * invocation of {@link KafkaConsumer#poll(java.time.Duration)} in which this callback is being executed. This means it is not\n181:      * necessary to catch these exceptions and re-attempt to wakeup or interrupt the consumer thread.\n182:      *\n183:      * @param partitions Partitions that have been added to the assignment as a result of the rebalance.\n184:      *                   Note that partitions that were already owned by this consumer and remain assigned are not\n185:      *                   included in this list under the Classic/Cooperative or Consumer protocols. THe full assignment\n186:      *                   will be received under the Classic/Eager protocol.\n187:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException    If raised from a nested call to {@link KafkaConsumer}\n188:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException If raised from a nested call to {@link KafkaConsumer}\n189:      */\n190:     void onPartitionsAssigned(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions);\n191: \n192:     /**\n193:      * A callback method you can implement to provide handling of cleaning up resources for partitions that have already\n194:      * been reassigned to other consumers. This method will not be called during normal execution as the owned partitions would\n195:      * first be revoked by calling the {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener#onPartitionsRevoked}, before being reassigned\n196:      * to other consumers during a rebalance event. However, during exceptional scenarios when the consumer realized that it\n197:      * does not own this partition any longer, i.e. not revoked via a normal rebalance event, then this method would be invoked.\n198:      * <p>\n199:      * For example, this function is called if a consumer's session timeout has expired, or if a fatal error has been\n200:      * received indicating the consumer is no longer part of the group.\n201:      * <p>\n202:      * By default it will just trigger {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener#onPartitionsRevoked}; for users who want to distinguish\n203:      * the handling logic of revoked partitions v.s. lost partitions, they can override the default implementation.\n204:      * <p>\n205:      * It is possible\n206:      * for a {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException} or {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException}\n207:      * to be raised from one of these nested invocations. In this case, the exception will be propagated to the current\n208:      * invocation of {@link KafkaConsumer#poll(java.time.Duration)} in which this callback is being executed. This means it is not\n209:      * necessary to catch these exceptions and re-attempt to wakeup or interrupt the consumer thread.\n210:      *\n211:      * @param partitions The list of partitions that were assigned to the consumer and now have been reassigned\n212:      *                   to other consumers. Poll() Behavior and Blocking\n\n**Test: `testPollReturnsRecords(GroupProtocol groupProtocol)` - Line 438**\n- Verifies that `poll(Duration.ZERO)` with available records returns all records and updates next offsets\n- Key behavior: Returns 5 records with correct partition set and nextOffsets metadata\n\n**Test: `testPollThrowsInterruptExceptionIfInterrupted(GroupProtocol groupProtocol)` - Line 1437**\n- Demonstrates that `Thread.interrupt()` before poll() throws `InterruptException`\n- Important edge case: Thread interrupt state is preserved and can affect subsequent operations\n- Test ensures proper cleanup with `Thread.interrupted()` to avoid polluting subsequent tests\n\n**Test: `testPollWithNoSubscription(GroupProtocol groupProtocol)` - Line 1905**\n- Calling poll() without subscription or assignment throws `IllegalStateException`\n- Also applies to empty subscriptions and empty user assignments (lines 1912-1924)\n\n**Test: `testWakeupWithFetchDataAvailable(GroupProtocol groupProtocol)` - Line 1396**\n- Non-obvious behavior: After `wakeup()` throws `WakeupException`, position is NOT advanced\n- Next poll() after wakeup() returns the buffered data correctly\n- Critical semantic: Wakeup interrupts the current poll without advancing offset state\n\n### 2. Offset Commit Strategies (Sync vs Async)\n\n**Test: `testCommitSync()` - CommitRequestManagerTest.java:296**\n- Synchronous commit sends request and waits for coordinator response\n- Verifies `unsentOffsetCommitRequests()` has exactly 1 request\n- Metadata is updated with epoch information via `updateLastSeenEpochIfNewer()`\n- Future completes with exact offsets that were committed\n\n**Test: `testCommitSyncWithEmptyOffsets()` - CommitRequestManagerTest.java:322**\n- Empty offset maps complete immediately without sending requests\n- No coordinator interaction needed\n\n**Test: `testCommitAsync()` - CommitRequestManagerTest.java:338**\n- Async commits also send request immediately (non-obvious: not truly async in sending)\n- Future completes when response received\n- Semantic difference from sync: Returns immediately after queueing, not after response\n\n**Test: `testOffsetCommitAsyncFailedWithRetriableThrowsRetriableCommitException()` - CommitRequestManagerTest.java:1072**\n- Critical behavior: Async commits with retriable errors (e.g., `COORDINATOR_NOT_AVAILABLE`) are NOT retried\n- Returns `RetriableCommitFailedException` to caller\n- Edge case: Differs from sync behavior which may retry\n\n**Test: `testOffsetCommitSingleFailedAttemptPerRequestWhenPartitionErrors()` - CommitRequestManagerTest.java:1097**\n- Subtle behavior: Multiple partition-level errors in one response count as single attempt\n- Retriable exceptions get retried with backoff (2+ attempts observed)\n- Non-retriable errors are not retried\n\n**Test: `testAutoCommitSentBeforePositionUpdate(GroupProtocol groupProtocol)` - Line 1296**\n- Critical timing semantic: Auto-commits happen BEFORE position is updated to user\n- User hasn't consumed records yet when auto-commit fires at interval boundary\n- Ensures committed offset reflects actual consumed data, not lookahead\n\n**Test: `testCommitSyncAllConsumed()` - Non-Obvious Behavioral Edge Cases\n\n**Wakeup Semantics:**\n- `testWakeupBeforeCallingPoll()` - Wakeup before poll throws immediately\n- `testWakeupAfterEmptyFetch()` - Wakeup can occur during fetch collection\n- `testWakeupAfterNonEmptyFetch()` - Wakeup interrupts data return\n- Key insight: Wakeup does NOT advance position\n\n**Poll State Validation:**\n- Empty assignment throws IllegalStateException (even with empty set)\n- Only subscription+assignment (non-empty) or manual assign allows polling\n\n**Callback Exception Propagation:**\n- Framework wraps non-KafkaException as `KafkaException`\n- Original cause preserved in wrapped exception\n- Assignment state updates despite exceptions\n\n**Coordinator Discovery:**\n- `FindCoordinatorResponse` requests happen before any offset operations\n- Coordinator election may fail (separate exception types)\n- Retry logic with backoff applied\n\n**Metadata Updates:**\n- Epoch information from responses updates consumer metadata\n- Important for conflict resolution with other consumers\n\n## Summary of Key Insights\n\n1. **Position doesn't advance on wakeup** - critical for exactly-once semantics\n2. **Auto-commit happens BEFORE user sees records** - timing sensitive\n3. **State updates despite callback exceptions** - resilient design\n4. **Seek overrides pending resets** - user intent takes precedence\n5. **Async commit doesn't retry on retriable errors** - different from sync behavior\n6. **Multiple partition errors = single attempt** - batching semantics\n7. **Deserialization error stops position** - This can be useful (for example) to store information about which\n28:  * node made the commit, what time the commit was made, etc.\n29:  */\n30: public class OffsetAndMetadata implements Serializable {\n31:     private static final long serialVersionUID = 2019555404968089681L;\n32: \n33:     private final long offset;\n34:     private final String metadata;\n35: \n36:     // We use null to represent the absence of a leader epoch to simplify serialization.\n37:     // I.e., older serializations of this class which do not have this field will automatically\n38:     // initialize its value to null.\n39:     private final Integer leaderEpoch;\n40: \n41:     /**\n42:      * Construct a new OffsetAndMetadata object for committing through {@link KafkaConsumer}.\n43:      *\n44:      * @param offset The offset to be committed\n45:      * @param leaderEpoch Optional leader epoch of the last consumed record\n46:      * @param metadata Non-null metadata\n47:      */\n48:     public OffsetAndMetadata(long offset, Optional<Integer> leaderEpoch, String metadata) {\n49:         if (offset < 0)\n50:             throw new IllegalArgumentException(\"Invalid negative offset\");\n51: \n52:         this.offset = offset;\n53:         this.leaderEpoch = leaderEpoch.orElse(null);\n54: \n55:         // The server converts null metadata to an empty string. So we store it as an empty string as well on the client\n56:         // to be consistent.\n57:         this.metadata = Objects.requireNonNullElse(metadata, OffsetFetchResponse.NO_METADATA);\n58:     }\n59: \n60:     /**\n61:      * Construct a new OffsetAndMetadata object for committing through {@link KafkaConsumer}.\n62:      * @param offset The offset to be committed\n63:      * @param metadata Non-null metadata\n64:      */\n65:     public OffsetAndMetadata(long offset, String metadata) {\n66:         this(offset, Optional.empty(), metadata);\n67:     }\n68: \n69:     /**\n70:      * Construct a new OffsetAndMetadata object for committing through {@link KafkaConsumer}. The metadata\n71:      * associated with the commit will be empty.\n72:      * @param offset The offset to be committed\n73:      */\n74:     public OffsetAndMetadata(long offset) {\n75:         this(offset, \"\");\n76:     }\n77: \n78:     public long offset() {\n79:         return offset;\n80:     }\n81: \n82:     /**\n83:      * Get the metadata of the previously consumed record.\n84:      *\n85:      * @return the metadata or empty string if no metadata\n86:      */\n87:     public String metadata() {\n88:         return metadata;\n89:     }\n90: \n91:     /**\n92:      * Get the leader epoch of the previously consumed record (if one is known). Log truncation is detected\n93:      * if there exists a leader epoch which is larger than this epoch and begins at an offset earlier than\n94:      * the committed offset.\n95:      *\n96:      * @return the leader epoch or empty if not known\n97:      */\n98:     public Optional<Integer> leaderEpoch() {\n99:         if (leaderEpoch == null || leaderEpoch < 0)\n100:             return Optional.empty();\n101:         return Optional.of(leaderEpoch);\n102:     }\n103: \n104:     @Override\n105:     public boolean equals(Object o) {\n106:         if (this == o) return true;\n107:         if (o == null || getClass() != o.getClass()) return false;\n108:         OffsetAndMetadata that = (OffsetAndMetadata) o;\n109:         return offset == that.offset &&\n110:                 Objects.equals(metadata, that.metadata) &&\n111:                 Objects.equals(leaderEpoch(), that.leaderEpoch());\n112:     }\n113: \n114:     @Override\n115:     public int hashCode() {\n116:         return Objects.hash(offset, metadata, leaderEpoch());\n117:     }\n118: \n119:     @Override\n120:     public String toString() {\n121:         return \"OffsetAndMetadata{\" +\n122:                 \"offset=\" + offset +\n123:                 \", leaderEpoch=\" + leaderEpoch().orElse(null) +\n124:                 \", metadata='\" + metadata + '\\'' +\n125:                 '}';\n126:     }\n127: }\n128: ","url":"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-benchmarks/kafka--e678b4b@47ac12bd6042edf23f1dbca3d789db945d62802d/-/blob/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/consumer/OffsetAndMetadata.java"}}}

instanceId;\n71:         this.readCommitted = readCommitted;\n72:         this.numRecords = numRecords;\n73:         this.remainingRecords = numRecords;\n74:         this.latch = latch;\n75:     }\n76: \n77:     @Override\n78:     public void run() {\n79:         // the consumer instance is NOT thread safe\n80:         try (KafkaConsumer<Integer, String> consumer = createKafkaConsumer()) {\n81:             // subscribes to a list of topics to get dynamically assigned partitions\n82:             // this class implements the rebalance listener that we pass here to be notified of such events\n83:             consumer.subscribe(singleton(topic), this);\n84:             Utils.printOut(\"Subscribed to %s\", topic);\n85:             while (!closed && remainingRecords > 0) {\n86:                 try {\n87:                     // if required, poll updates partition assignment and invokes the configured rebalance listener\n88:                     // then tries to fetch records sequentially using the last committed offset or auto.offset.reset policy\n89:                     // returns immediately if there are records or times out returning an empty record set\n90:                     // the next poll must be called within session.timeout.ms to avoid group rebalance\n91:                     ConsumerRecords<Integer, String> records = consumer.poll(Duration.ofSeconds(1));\n92:                     for (ConsumerRecord<Integer, String> record : records) {\n93:                         Utils.maybePrintRecord(numRecords, record);\n94:                     }\n95:                     remainingRecords -= records.count();\n96:                 } catch (AuthorizationException | UnsupportedVersionException\n97:                          | RecordDeserializationException e) {\n98:                     // we can't recover from these exceptions\n99:                     Utils.printErr(e.getMessage());\n100:                     shutdown();\n101:                 } catch (OffsetOutOfRangeException | NoOffsetForPartitionException e) {\n102:                     // invalid or no offset found without auto.reset.policy\n103:                     Utils.printOut(\"Invalid or no offset found, using latest\");\n104:                     consumer.seekToEnd(e.partitions());\n105:                     consumer.commitSync();\n106:                 } catch (KafkaException e) {\n107:                     // log the exception and try to continue\n108:                     Utils.printErr(e.getMessage());\n109:                 }\n110:             }\n111:         } catch (Throwable e) {\n112:             Utils.printErr(\"Unhandled exception\");\n113:             e.printStackTrace();\n114:         }\n115:         Utils.printOut(\"Fetched %d records\", numRecords - remainingRecords);\n116:         shutdown();\n117:     }\n118: \n119:     public void shutdown() {\n120:         if (!closed) {\n121:             closed = true;\n122:             latch.countDown();\n123:         }\n124:     }\n125: \n126:     public KafkaConsumer<Integer, String> createKafkaConsumer() {\n127:         Properties props = new Properties();\n128:         // bootstrap server config is required for consumer to connect to brokers\n129:         props.put(ConsumerConfig.BOOTSTRAP_SERVERS_CONFIG, bootstrapServers);\n130:         // client id is not required, but it's good to track the source of requests beyond just ip/port\n131:         // by allowing a logical application name to be included in server-side request logging\n132:         props.put(ConsumerConfig.CLIENT_ID_CONFIG, \"client-\" + UUID.randomUUID());\n133:         // consumer group id is required when we use subscribe(topics) for group management\n134:         props.put(ConsumerConfig.GROUP_ID_CONFIG, groupId);\n135:         // sets static membership to improve availability (e.g. rolling restart)\n136:         instanceId.ifPresent(id -> props.put(ConsumerConfig.GROUP_INSTANCE_ID_CONFIG, id));\n137:         // disables auto commit when EOS is enabled, because offsets are committed with the transaction\n138:         props.put(ConsumerConfig.ENABLE_AUTO_COMMIT_CONFIG, readCommitted ? \"false\" : \"true\");\n139:         // key and value are just byte arrays, so we need to set appropriate deserializers\n140:         props.put(ConsumerConfig.KEY_DESERIALIZER_CLASS_CONFIG, IntegerDeserializer.class);\n141:         props.put(ConsumerConfig.VALUE_DESERIALIZER_CLASS_CONFIG, StringDeserializer.class);\n142:         if (readCommitted) {\n143:             // skips ongoing and aborted transactions\n144:             props.put(ConsumerConfig.ISOLATION_LEVEL_CONFIG, \"read_committed\");\n145:         }\n146:         // sets the reset offset policy in case of invalid or no offset\n147:         props.put(ConsumerConfig.AUTO_OFFSET_RESET_CONFIG, \"earliest\");\n148:         return new KafkaConsumer<>(props);\n149:     }\n150: \n151:     @Override\n152:     public void onPartitionsRevoked(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions) {\n153:         Utils.printOut(\"Revoked partitions: %s\", partitions);\n154:         // partitions) {\n159:         Utils.printOut(\"Assigned partitions: %s\", partitions);\n160:         // this can be used to read the offsets from an external store or some other initialization\n161:     }\n162: \n163:     @Override\n164:     public void onPartitionsLost(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions) {\n165:         Utils.printOut(\"Lost partitions: %s\", partitions);\n166:         // this is {"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_01Ho5q3P1dRQn6nodWLrF6L3","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"tool_use","id":"toolu_018XcVkabKsKRjRPK5pawcUC","name":"Write","input":{"file_path":"/workspace/documentation.md","content":"# Apache Kafka KafkaConsumer API Reference\n\n**Package:** `org.apache.kafka.clients.consumer`\n\n**Version:** Based on Apache Kafka client library (this documentation covers KafkaConsumer behavioral semantics and complete API surface)\n\n## Table of Contents\n\n1. [Overview](#overview)\n2. [Core Types](#core-types)\n3. [Consumer Lifecycle](#consumer-lifecycle)\n4. [Subscription and Assignment](#subscription-and-assignment)\n5. [Polling and Data Fetching](#polling-and-data-fetching)\n6. [Offset Management](#offset-management)\n7. [Flow Control and Position Management](#flow-control-and-position-management)\n8. [Consumer Group Mechanics](#consumer-group-mechanics)\n9. [Rebalancing and Callbacks](#rebalancing-and-callbacks)\n10. [Metadata and Monitoring](#metadata-and-monitoring)\n11. [Error Handling](#error-handling)\n12. [Configuration-Driven Behaviors](#configuration-driven-behaviors)\n13. [Usage Examples](#usage-examples)\n14. [Best Practices and Patterns](#best-practices-and-patterns)\n\n---\n\n## Overview\n\n### What is KafkaConsumer?\n\n`KafkaConsumer` is a client for consuming records from Apache Kafka topics. It handles:\n\n- **Cluster membership**: Automatically maintains TCP connections to broker nodes\n- **Broker failure recovery**: Transparently adapts when brokers fail or partition leaders change\n- **Consumer group coordination**: Works with the Kafka broker's group coordination protocol for distributed, fault-tolerant consumption\n- **Offset management**: Tracks read position and supports automatic or manual offset commits\n- **Load balancing**: Partitions are automatically distributed across group members\n\n### Key Characteristics\n\n- **NOT thread-safe**: All operations must occur on a single thread\n- **Resource-intensive**: Maintains broker connections; must be explicitly closed\n- **Event-driven**: Uses `poll()` as the main entry point for all operations\n- **Transparent rebalancing**: Consumer group membership changes trigger automatic rebalancing via `poll()`\n\n---\n\n## Core Types\n\n### KafkaConsumer<K, V>\n\nThe main consumer class implementing the `Consumer<K, V>` interface.\n\n**Type parameters:**\n- `K`: Type of message keys (e.g., `String`, `byte[]`, custom classes)\n- `V`: Type of message values (e.g., `String`, `byte[]`, custom classes)\n\n### Consumer<K, V> Interface\n\nBase interface that KafkaConsumer implements. Useful for testing and mocking.\n\n```java\npublic interface Consumer<K, V> extends Closeable {\n    // Subscription/assignment\n    Set<TopicPartition> assignment();\n    Set<String> subscription();\n    void subscribe(Collection<String> topics);\n    void subscribe(Collection<String> topics, ConsumerRebalanceListener listener);\n    void subscribe(Pattern pattern);\n    void subscribe(Pattern pattern, ConsumerRebalanceListener listener);\n    void assign(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions);\n    void unsubscribe();\n\n    // Polling\n    ConsumerRecords<K, V> poll(Duration timeout);\n\n    // Offset commits\n    void commitSync();\n    void commitSync(Duration timeout);\n    void commitSync(Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndMetadata> offsets);\n    void commitSync(Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndMetadata> offsets, Duration timeout);\n    void commitAsync();\n    void commitAsync(OffsetCommitCallback callback);\n    void commitAsync(Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndMetadata> offsets, OffsetCommitCallback callback);\n\n    // Position and seeking\n    void seek(TopicPartition partition, long offset);\n    void seek(TopicPartition partition, OffsetAndMetadata offsetAndMetadata);\n    void seekToBeginning(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions);\n    void seekToEnd(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions);\n    long position(TopicPartition partition);\n    long position(TopicPartition partition, Duration timeout);\n\n    // Offset queries\n    Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndMetadata> committed(Set<TopicPartition> partitions);\n    Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndMetadata> committed(Set<TopicPartition> partitions, Duration timeout);\n    Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndTimestamp> offsetsForTimes(Map<TopicPartition, Long> timestampsToSearch);\n    Map<TopicPartition, Long> beginningOffsets(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions);\n    Map<TopicPartition, Long> endOffsets(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions);\n\n    // Flow control\n    void pause(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions);\n    void resume(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions);\n    Set<TopicPartition> paused();\n\n    // Metadata\n    List<PartitionInfo> partitionsFor(String topic);\n    Map<String, List<PartitionInfo>> listTopics();\n    Map<MetricName, ? extends Metric> metrics();\n    Uuid clientInstanceId(Duration timeout);\n\n    // Group management\n    ConsumerGroupMetadata groupMetadata();\n    void enforceRebalance();\n    void enforceRebalance(String reason);\n\n    // Lifecycle\n    void close();\n    void wakeup();\n}\n```\n\n### ConsumerRecords<K, V>\n\nContainer for consumer records returned by `poll()`.\n\n**Key methods:**\n- `List<ConsumerRecord<K, V>> records(TopicPartition partition)` - Get records for specific partition\n- `Iterable<ConsumerRecord<K, V>> records(String topic)` - Get records for specific topic\n- `Set<TopicPartition> partitions()` - Get all partitions in this record batch\n- `Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndMetadata> nextOffsets()` - Get next offsets after consuming these records\n- `int count()` - Total count of records across all partitions\n- `boolean isEmpty()` - Check if empty\n\n**Behavioral notes:**\n- Implements `Iterable<ConsumerRecord<K, V>>` for easy streaming\n- Contains metadata about next offsets to support automatic offset tracking\n- Records are immutable; iteration is safe from concurrent modification\n\n### ConsumerRecord<K, V>\n\nIndividual message record with metadata.\n\n**Key properties:**\n- `String topic()` - Topic name\n- `int partition()` - Partition number\n- `long offset()` - Message offset in partition\n- `K key()` - Message key (nullable)\n- `V value()` - Message value\n- `long timestamp()` - Timestamp (milliseconds since epoch)\n- `int serializedKeySize()` - Size of key in bytes\n- `int serializedValueSize()` - Size of value in bytes\n- `Headers headers()` - Message headers (if enabled)\n- `long leaderEpoch()` - Leader epoch at time of produce (useful for detecting log truncation)\n\n### OffsetAndMetadata\n\nRepresents a committed offset with optional metadata and leader epoch.\n\n```java\npublic class OffsetAndMetadata {\n    // Constructors\n    public OffsetAndMetadata(long offset)  // offset only\n    public OffsetAndMetadata(long offset, String metadata)  // with metadata\n    public OffsetAndMetadata(long offset, Optional<Integer> leaderEpoch, String metadata)  // full\n\n    // Methods\n    public long offset()\n    public String metadata()\n    public Optional<Integer> leaderEpoch()\n}\n```\n\n**Semantic notes:**\n- Metadata field is useful for tracking commit source or context\n- Leader epoch helps detect log truncation in broker\n- Negative offsets throw `IllegalArgumentException` at construction\n\n### ConsumerRebalanceListener\n\nInterface for rebalance callbacks during consumer group coordination.\n\n```java\npublic interface ConsumerRebalanceListener {\n    /**\n     * Called when partitions are revoked (normal end-of-ownership).\n     * Safe to commit offsets here via consumer.commitSync().\n     */\n    void onPartitionsRevoked(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions);\n\n    /**\n     * Called after rebalance completes with newly assigned partitions.\n     * Safe to initialize positions here via consumer.seek().\n     */\n    void onPartitionsAssigned(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions);\n\n    /**\n     * Called when partitions are lost without revocation opportunity\n     * (e.g., session timeout). DO NOT commit offsets here.\n     * Default implementation calls onPartitionsRevoked().\n     */\n    default void onPartitionsLost(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions) {\n        onPartitionsRevoked(partitions);\n    }\n}\n```\n\n**Callback execution:**\n- All callbacks execute on the consumer thread during `poll()` calls\n- Exceptions from callbacks propagate to the `poll()` caller\n- Order is: `onPartitionsRevoked()` → rebalance → `onPartitionsAssigned()`\n- `onPartitionsLost()` only called when partitions reassigned without revocation\n\n### OffsetCommitCallback\n\nCallback for asynchronous offset commits.\n\n```java\npublic interface OffsetCommitCallback {\n    /**\n     * Called when offset commit response received from broker.\n     * @param offsets Map of offsets committed (may be partial if error)\n     * @param exception Exception if commit failed, null if successful\n     */\n    void onComplete(Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndMetadata> offsets, Exception exception);\n}\n```\n\n---\n\n## Consumer Lifecycle\n\n### Creating a Consumer\n\n#### Constructor Variants\n\n```java\n// With Properties (string configurations)\nKafkaConsumer<String, String> consumer = new KafkaConsumer<>(\n    properties,\n    new StringDeserializer(),\n    new StringDeserializer()\n);\n\n// With Map<String, Object>\nKafkaConsumer<String, String> consumer = new KafkaConsumer<>(\n    configs,\n    new StringDeserializer(),\n    new StringDeserializer()\n);\n\n// Without explicit deserializers (from config)\nKafkaConsumer<String, String> consumer = new KafkaConsumer<>(properties);\n\n// Minimal\nKafkaConsumer<byte[], byte[]> consumer = new KafkaConsumer<>(properties);\n```\n\n**Minimum required configuration:**\n- `bootstrap.servers`: Initial broker addresses (e.g., \"localhost:9092\")\n- `key.deserializer`: Class name of key deserializer\n- `value.deserializer`: Class name of value deserializer\n- `group.id`: Consumer group name (for group-managed subscriptions)\n\n**Important notes:**\n- Consumer acquires resources immediately (broker connections)\n- All configuration keys are documented in `ConsumerConfig`\n- Passing deserializers directly bypasses `configure()` call from config\n\n#### Configuration Properties\n\nKey configuration properties that affect behavior:\n\n| Property | Default | Purpose |\n|----------|---------|---------|\n| `bootstrap.servers` | (required) | Comma-separated broker addresses |\n| `group.id` | \"\" | Consumer group ID (required for group management) |\n| `key.deserializer` | (required) | Key deserializer class |\n| `value.deserializer` | (required) | Value deserializer class |\n| `enable.auto.commit` | true | Enable automatic offset commits |\n| `auto.commit.interval.ms` | 5000 | Auto-commit interval (milliseconds) |\n| `session.timeout.ms` | 45000 | Session timeout before consumer is removed from group |\n| `heartbeat.interval.ms` | 10000 | Interval for sending heartbeats |\n| `max.poll.interval.ms` | 300000 | Max time between poll() calls before removal from group |\n| `max.poll.records` | 500 | Max records returned per poll() |\n| `auto.offset.reset` | \"latest\" | Offset policy when not found: \"earliest\", \"latest\", or \"none\" |\n| `isolation.level` | \"read_uncommitted\" | \"read_committed\" to skip uncommitted transaction messages |\n| `fetch.min.bytes` | 1 | Min bytes broker waits before responding to fetch |\n| `fetch.max.wait.ms` | 500 | Max time broker waits to accumulate fetch.min.bytes |\n\nSee the **Configuration-Driven Behaviors** section for how these affect API behavior.\n\n### Closing a Consumer\n\n```java\n// Simple close (default timeout from config)\nconsumer.close();\n\n// With explicit timeout\nconsumer.close(Duration.ofSeconds(30));\n\n// With close options\nconsumer.close(new CloseOptions().timeout(Duration.ofSeconds(30)));\n```\n\n**Important behaviors:**\n- `close()` without timeout uses `request.timeout.ms` from config\n- Triggers `onPartitionsRevoked()` callback if using group management\n- Blocks until all pending operations complete or timeout expires\n- Safe to call multiple times (idempotent)\n- Should be in try-with-resources or finally block\n\n**Resource cleanup:**\n- Closes broker connections\n- Stops heartbeat thread\n- Flushes pending offset commits (if auto-commit enabled)\n- Revokes all assigned partitions\n\n---\n\n## Subscription and Assignment\n\n### Dynamic Subscription (Group-Managed)\n\nConsumer group coordinator automatically manages partition assignment.\n\n```java\n// Subscribe to specific topics\nconsumer.subscribe(Arrays.asList(\"topic1\", \"topic2\"), listener);\n\n// Subscribe with rebalance listener (recommended)\nconsumer.subscribe(\n    Arrays.asList(\"orders\", \"returns\"),\n    new ConsumerRebalanceListener() {\n        @Override\n        public void onPartitionsRevoked(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions) {\n            // Save offsets for graceful shutdown\n            consumer.commitSync();\n        }\n\n        @Override\n        public void onPartitionsAssigned(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions) {\n            // Initialize any state needed for new partitions\n        }\n    }\n);\n\n// Get current subscription\nSet<String> topics = consumer.subscription();\n```\n\n**Semantics:**\n- Topics can be added/removed dynamically between polls\n- Subscription change triggers rebalance on next `poll()`\n- Regex subscription updates topic list on each `poll()` based on broker metadata\n- Only one subscription mode per consumer (subscribe OR assign)\n\n### Manual Assignment (Standalone)\n\nConsumer directly controls partition assignment without group coordination.\n\n```java\n// Manually assign partitions\nconsumer.assign(Arrays.asList(\n    new TopicPartition(\"orders\", 0),\n    new TopicPartition(\"orders\", 1),\n    new TopicPartition(\"orders\", 2)\n));\n\n// Get current assignment\nSet<TopicPartition> partitions = consumer.assignment();\n\n// Unsubscribe/unassign (resets to empty)\nconsumer.unsubscribe();\n```\n\n**Behavioral differences from subscription:**\n- No rebalancing occurs\n- No group coordination\n- Partitions never reassigned unless manually changed\n- Consumer doesn't heartbeat to broker\n- No `max.poll.interval.ms` enforcement\n- Useful for standalone consumers or precise partition
